SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's central claim—that semi-supervised CNN-based selection improves SMT adaptation with tiny in-domain data—is supported by multi-language experiments reporting up to 3.1 BLEU gains and significance testing via bootstrap resampling (Section 3). The methodology uses reasonable baselines (ngram, rnnlm, comblm) and describes the selection procedure (train CNN on small in-domain positives and sampled negatives, score general-domain sentences). However, there are no formal proofs or mathematical derivations, and some experimental choices are only described at a high level (e.g. number of selected sentences is empirically determined), leaving minor gaps in reproducibility and completeness of evidence.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The contribution is an application of existing ideas (one-hot and semi-supervised CNNs, and pre-trained word embeddings) to the specific problem of bilingual training-data selection for SMT with very small in-domain samples. The paper acknowledges closely related prior work (Johnson and Zhang, Duh et al., Axelrod et al.) and frames the novelty as using semi-supervised CNNs for selection, which is a useful but incremental extension rather than a brand-new modeling paradigm.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized into standard sections (related work, method, experiments, discussion, conclusion) and key ideas like one-hot CNN and semi-supervised setup are explained (Section 3.1, 4). However, the provided text contains many repeated paragraphs and some editorial issues, and several implementation/details sections are described at a high level rather than with fully consistent notation, which makes parts harder to follow.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Experiments cover four language directions and multiple social-media domains, and comparisons to three strong LM-based selection baselines are reported with statistical testing. The paper also reports robustness to tiny in-domain sizes (as few as 100 sentences). Still, it lacks deeper ablation studies (e.g. explicit comparison of one-hot vs. embedding inputs, detailed sensitivity analyses, or more exploration of negative-sampling choices) and limited discussion of failure cases or detailed hyperparameter settings.

IMPACT.justification: Adapting MT with only tiny in-domain data is an important practical problem, particularly for social-media domains; showing consistent BLEU gains (up to 3.1) across multiple language pairs suggests useful applicability. While the work is unlikely to be transformative theoretically, it is likely to be adopted by practitioners and to stimulate follow-up work on fine-grained and low-resource adaptation methods.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic—domain adaptation for SMT using neural text classifiers—and the methods (CNNs, word2vec, comparisons to LM-based selection) are squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. The paper uses standard evaluation metrics and baselines expected by the community and addresses an audience interested in both MT and neural text classification.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against established LM-based selection baselines (ngram, rnnlm, comblm) and follows previous setups (citing Duh et al.), and uses bootstrap resampling for significance. That makes comparisons fair for the targeted class of baselines, though it omits empirical comparisons against other non-LM adaptation approaches (e.g. instance weighting, VSM/mixture-model variants) despite citing them, which would strengthen the appraisal of relative gains.

REPLICABILITY.justification: Datasets used are publicly available and the paper names toolkits and approaches (word2vec, RNNLM toolkit, one-hot vs. semi-supervised CNN). Nonetheless, many important details are missing from the provided text (precise CNN architecture/hyperparameters, training epochs, selection thresholds, exact sizes of selected data per experiment), so full reproducibility from the paper alone is only partial.
